policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.
The following table outlines the necessary fields and their values for various claim types to identify 340B-
purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B pharmacy and medical benefit claims billed to NYS Medicaid
as primary payor require identifiers and actual acquisi
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member. There are two
methods available for utilization:
• The Automated Response Unit (ARU or telephone).
• The ePACES web-based application.
These systems enable pharmacy providers to quickly verify eligibility and facilitate electronic submission of claims.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Recipient Restriction Program
Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services
from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied. Medicaid members who are
restricted to a primary Durable Medical Equipment (DME) dealer must receive all DME and prosthetic and orthotic
appliances from the DME provider.
All primary pharmacy providers must maintain a patient profile for each restricted Medicaid member. The profile must
contain, at a minimum, the member’s name, and the date the drugs or supplies were dispensed. These profiles must
be made readily available to the New York State Department of Health or its agents, upon request.
When a Medicaid member is restricted to an ordering practitioner (physician, clinic, inpatient hospital and/ or dentist),
all pharmacy services must be ordered by the primary medical provider (clinic or MD) with the Medicaid member’s
restriction type.
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_1.answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, including billing requirements and eligibility verification.
* Provider manual documents outline specific procedures for pharmacy services, including claim submission and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid dual eligible pharmacy claims are subject to specific billing requirements and eligibility verification. Providers must verify the eligibility of Medicaid members before submitting claims. Claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drugs billed to NYRx or Medicaid Managed Care plans. Providers can access individual plan information via the NYS MMC Pharmacy Benefit Information Center web page.

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.
[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [6]]: "all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of Medicaid members using the Automated Response Unit (ARU) or ePACES web-based application. Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [10]]: "Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs..."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require eligibility verification for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed procedures for claim submission and recipient restriction programs.
* Medicaid policy documents do not explicitly state procedures for claim submission, but rather refer providers to contact individual plans.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Caveats**

Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for Provider Manual.
test_id_1.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_1.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_1.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

policy.text:
article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to
incorrect or missing claim level identifiers, the CE is responsible for reimbursing manufacturers the
340B discounts.

purchased drug claims. Please note: 340B pharmacy and medical benefit claims billed to NYS Medicaid
as primary payor require identifiers and actual acquisi
policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member. There are two
methods available for utilization:
• The Automated Response Unit (ARU or telephone).
• The ePACES web-based application.
These systems enable pharmacy providers to quickly verify eligibility and facilitate electronic submission of claims.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Recipient Restriction Program
Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services
from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied. Medicaid members who are
restricted to a primary Durable Medical Equipment (DME) dealer must receive all DME and prosthetic and orthotic
appliances from the DME provider.
All primary pharmacy providers must maintain a patient profile for each restricted Medicaid member. The profile must
contain, at a minimum, the member’s name, and the date the drugs or supplies were dispensed. These profiles must
be made readily available to the New York State Department of Health or its agents, upon request.
When a Medicaid member is restricted to an ordering practitioner (physician, clinic, inpatient hospital and/ or dentist),
all pharmacy services must be ordered by the primary medical provider (clinic or MD) with the Medicaid member’s
restriction type.
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_3.answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, including billing requirements and eligibility verification.
* Provider manual documents outline specific procedures for pharmacy services, including claim submission and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid dual eligible pharmacy claims require providers to verify eligibility using the Automated Response Unit (ARU) or ePACES web-based application. Providers must also obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drug claims billed to NYRx or Medicaid Managed Care plans.

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services must verify the eligibility of Medicaid members using the Automated Response Unit (ARU) or ePACES web-based application. Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [10]]: "Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs..."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require eligibility verification for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed procedures for claim submission and recipient restriction programs.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [10]]: "Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs..."
[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.

**Caveats**

Insufficient grounded evidence in the provided documents to define this concept for Provider Manual.
test_id_3.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_3.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_3.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

https://mmcdruginformation.nysdoh.suny.edu/, then select the individual plan and choose “Pharmacy
Vaccine Billing Guidance”.
Reimbursement for these vaccines may be based on a patient specific, or non-patient specific, order. These
orders must be kept on file at the pharmacy. The ordering prescriber must be actively enrolled as a NYS
Medicaid provider, unless otherwise exempt, and the prescribers National Provider Identifier (NPI) is required
on the claim for the claim to be paid.
Vaccines for administration to individuals under the age of 19 are recommended by the Advisory Committee
on Immunization Practices (ACIP) and are provided to Medicaid FFS members and MMC enrollees free of
charge by the Vaccines for Childre

article is intended to advise
providers, who may be new to submitting claims to NYRx, the NYS Medicaid Pharmacy program, that
all NCPDP-submitted 340B-purchased drug claims when NYS Medicaid is primary payor must be
submitted with claim level identifiers and at actual acquisition cost.
Claim level identifiers are required for any 340B-purchased drug billed to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care
(MMC) Plan, or as a secondary claim, whether it is a medical or pharmacy claim. It is the responsibility of the
covered entity (CE) and their contracted pharmacies, if applicable, to correctly identify claims dispensed with
the 340B stock of the CE for 340B-eligible NYS Medicaid patients to ensure rebates are not collected for
these drugs. If rebates are received by the NYS DOH for drugs obtained via the 340B program due to

340B discounts.

policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Eligibility
Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services are required to verify the eligibility of the Medicaid member. There are two
methods available for utilization:
• The Automated Response Unit (ARU or telephone).
• The ePACES web-based application.
These systems enable pharmacy providers to quickly verify eligibility and facilitate electronic submission of claims.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Recipient Restriction Program
Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services
from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied. Medicaid members who are
restricted to a primary Durable Medical Equipment (DME) dealer must receive all DME and prosthetic and orthotic
appliances from the DME provider.
All primary pharmacy providers must maintain a patient profile for each restricted Medicaid member. The profile must
contain, at a minimum, the member’s name, and the date the drugs or supplies were dispensed. These profiles must
be made readily available to the New York State Department of Health or its agents, upon request.
When a Medicaid member is restricted to an ordering practitioner (physician, clinic, inpatient hospital and/ or dentist),
all pharmacy services must be ordered by the primary medical provider (clinic or MD) with the Medicaid member’s
restriction type.
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_5.answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on dual eligible pharmacy claims, including billing requirements and eligibility verification.
* Provider manual documents outline specific procedures for pharmacy services, including claim submission and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid dual eligible pharmacy claims require providers to verify eligibility using the Automated Response Unit (ARU) or ePACES web-based application. Providers must also obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies. Claim level identifiers are required for 340B-purchased drug claims billed to NYRx or Medicaid Managed Care plans.

[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Pharmacy providers of Medicaid services must verify the eligibility of Medicaid members using the Automated Response Unit (ARU) or ePACES web-based application. Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [10]]: "Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs..."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require eligibility verification for dual eligible pharmacy claims.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed procedures for claim submission and recipient restriction programs.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [10]]: "Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs..."
[New York State Medicaid Update August 2021 Volume 37 Number 10 — pages [9]]: "providers must contact the individual plan" for Medicaid Managed Care Organization (MCO) billing guidance.

**Caveats**

Insufficient evidence is found in the Provider Manual regarding the specific requirements for submitting claims to NYRx, Medicaid Managed Care plans, or as a secondary claim.
test_id_5.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
"Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a ""03"" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection."
test_id_5.pm_ground_truth:
For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Medicaid may pay for certain drugs that are excluded from Medicare Part D coverage (e.g., specific OTCs, barbiturates, or benzodiazepines). The claim must be submitted to Medicaid with the appropriate rejection codes from the Part D plan.
test_id_5.policy_ground_truth:
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.
Pharmacies must include specific Submission Clarification Codes and a "03" in the Basis of Cost Determination field when billing Medicaid for these Part D-excluded drugs. This provides the exact billing data required, which is a level of detail not present in the manual itself, and is essential for avoiding claim rejection.

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.

contain, at a minimum, the member’s name, and the date the drugs or supplies were dispensed. These profiles must
be made readily available to the New York State Department of Health or its agents, upon request.
When a Medicaid member is restricted to an ordering practitioner (physician, clinic, inpatient hospital and/ or dentist),

A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either

General Policy document, located at: https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/PDFS/
Information_for_All_Providers-General_Policy.pdf. This includes adherence to all federal and NYS laws, rules

upon the ownership of the pharmacy and upon a licensed pharmacist who is designated by the owner as the

provider_manual.text:
Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Recipient Restriction Program
Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services
from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied. Medicaid members who are
restricted to a primary Durable Medical Equipment (DME) dealer must receive all DME and prosthetic and orthotic
appliances from the DME provider.
All primary pharmacy providers must maintain a patient profile for each restricted Medicaid member. The profile must
contain, at a minimum, the member’s name, and the date the drugs or supplies were dispensed. These profiles must
be made readily available to the New York State Department of Health or its agents, upon request.
When a Medicaid member is restricted to an ordering practitioner (physician, clinic, inpatient hospital and/ or dentist),
all pharmacy services must be ordered by the primary medical provider (clinic or MD) with the Medicaid member’s
restriction type.
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_7.answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.
* Provider manual documents outline procedures for handling third-party liability and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.

[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [6, 7]]: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is determined by the responsible third party. If a member has an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing NYRx as primary payor.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require responsible third parties to pay as primary payors before submitting a claim to Medicaid.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed information on handling Workers’ Compensation cases and recipient restriction programs.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [6, 7]]: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program."

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence is found in the Provider Manual regarding the specific requirements for handling third-party liability and primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_7.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_7.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_7.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_8.question: How do Medicaid and provider manual guidance define primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims?
test_id_8.concept: primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims

Third Party Liability
Federal regulations require that all available resources be used before Medicaid considers payment. Medicaid
regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require a provider to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim
to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as
primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first. Failure to obtain prior authorization does not overcome the
responsibility of the primary payor.
Claims for members with other coverage will be denied at point of service with National Council for Prescription Drug
Programs (NCPDP) response code/message “717- Client Has Other Insurance”. Through coordination of benefits,
Medicaid will pay the patient responsibility for a correctly submitted Medicaid coverable claim or will pay up to the
Medicaid allowed amount for a drug in a class specifically excluded from being covered under the Third-Party Liability
Plan (TPL) including Medicare.
Claims submitted to NYS Medicaid with a “no reimbursement” from any TPL must correctly be submitted. Pharmacy
providers who receive a “no TPL reimbursement” must ensure that the claim was adjudicated within the TPL’s claim
process requirements. This includes submitting the claim under the appropriate coverage benefit, medical or
pharmacy, such as with the Healthcare Common Procedure Coding System (HCPCS) or by the National Drug Code
(NDC). Additionally, pharmacies not contracted with the member’s TPL Plan may need to attempt an override with the
plan, or enroll with the plan, or advise the member and prescriber of the need to change dispensing to a network
pharmacy.
It is the responsibility of the prescriber to prescribe, per the plan’s formulary, and to pursue any claim issues such as,
but not limited to:
• Necessary prior authorizations or appeals, or
• Prescription alternatives (for a non-formulary or non-preferred drug) or
• Necessary changes (quantity or day supply, etc.) or
• Selecting an in-network pharmacy that is agreeable to the member, or
• Billing the appropriate benefit (medical vs. pharmacy or HCPCS vs NDC).
Pharmacies have a corresponding responsibility to submit claims to a Medicaid member’s other coverage per the
plan’s requirements before submitting the claim to Medicaid. Pharmacies that have difficulty in billing a plan should
advise the prescriber and member of potential delays and options for resolution. If the claim issues are resolved, the
pharmacy may then resubmit the claim to Medicaid after the claim is properly adjudicated with the TPL.
Submitting a TPL covered claim that bypasses the TPL’s responsibility of payment is considered fraudulent billing and is
subject to audit. Reminder: Providers must retain evidence that the responsible TPL denied the claim, other TPL
responses where applicable, and payment information was retained, for a minimum of six years following the date of
Medicaid payment.
NOTE:
• The Medicaid payment must be accepted as payment in full.
• Obtaining a clinical PA from the Prime Therapeutics State Government Solutions Call Center does not negate
the requirement to obtain payment from responsible third-party payor.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Workers’ Compensation and Coordination of Benefits
If a member has a work-related injury, for which they have an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’
compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program.
If the drug claim is NOT related to a member’s Workers’ Compensation case, such as a maintenance medication for an
unrelated chronic condition, NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field
309-C9. Medicaid is the payer of last resort, and providers must ensure all other responsible payers are included on the
claim, if applicable.
The Workers’ Compensation Board can be accessed here: https://www.wcb.ny.gov/content/main/hcpp/hcpp.jsp.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Recipient Restriction Program
Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services
from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied. Medicaid members who are
restricted to a primary Durable Medical Equipment (DME) dealer must receive all DME and prosthetic and orthotic
appliances from the DME provider.
All primary pharmacy providers must maintain a patient profile for each restricted Medicaid member. The profile must
contain, at a minimum, the member’s name, and the date the drugs or supplies were dispensed. These profiles must
be made readily available to the New York State Department of Health or its agents, upon request.
When a Medicaid member is restricted to an ordering practitioner (physician, clinic, inpatient hospital and/ or dentist),
all pharmacy services must be ordered by the primary medical provider (clinic or MD) with the Medicaid member’s
restriction type.
A primary physician or primary clinic is responsible for providing all medical care to the restricted recipient, either
directly or through referral of such recipient to another medical provider for appropriate services. If the primary
provider refers the Medicaid restricted recipient to another provider for services, the primary provider’s Medicaid
identification number must be used in the referring field to bill for those services. When dispensing medications
prescribed by the ‘referred’ provider, the pharmacy must enter the primary provider’s Medicaid identification number
in the referring field in the pharmacy claim.
Medicaid members may have durable medical equipment restrictions separate from pharmacy restrictions.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_9.answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents emphasize the importance of pursuing available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid.
* Provider manual documents outline specific procedures for handling third-party liability and recipient restriction programs.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid regulations at 18NYCRR §540.6(e) require providers to pursue any available other coverage prior to submitting a claim to Medicaid. If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first.

[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [6, 7]]: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program."

**Provider Manual Definition**

Primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims is determined by the responsible third party. If a member has an open Workers’ Compensation case, Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing NYRx as primary payor.

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [40, 41]]: "Medicaid members who have been assigned to a designated pharmacy are required to receive all pharmacy services from the selected pharmacy provider. All claims from other pharmacies will be denied."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "NYRx may be billed as the primary payer with an Eligibility Clarification Code of “2” in field 309-C9."

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources require responsible third parties to pay as primary payors before submitting a claim to Medicaid.
* Provider manual documents provide more detailed information on handling Workers’ Compensation cases and recipient restriction programs.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Update June 2023 Volume 39 Number 11 — pages [6, 7]]: "If there is a responsible third party who should be paying for the Medicaid member’s health benefits as primary payor, that responsible third party must pay first."
[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines — pages [17]]: "Workers’ compensation coverage must be utilized prior to billing the NYRx Medicaid Pharmacy Program."

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence is found in the Provider Manual regarding the specific requirements for handling third-party liability and primary payer responsibility for dual-eligible pharmacy claims.
test_id_9.ground_truth:
"For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the ""payer of last resort."" Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare."
test_id_9.pm_ground_truth: For dual-eligible members, the pharmacy must first bill Medicare Part D. It states that Medicaid is the "payer of last resort." Claims submitted to Medicaid first for drugs that are coverable by a member's Part D plan will be denied.
test_id_9.policy_ground_truth: This applies even to over-the-counter (OTC) drugs if they are covered by the member's Part D plan. A pharmacy cannot simply bill Medicaid for a covered OTC drug without first billing Medicare.

test_id_11.question: What are the differences between Medicaid policy and provider manual guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members?

compare_sections.headline_summary:
Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.
Provider manual documents outline procedures for handling co-payments and medical supplies.
compare_sections.policy_definition:
Medicaid regulations do not explicitly define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. However, [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11] -[Chunk ID: mu_no11_oct24_pr.pdf::0031]-[pages: [12]] states that services provided to dual-eligible members are not eligible for incentive payments.
[Document Title: Children�s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children�s Home and Community Based Services Manual] -[Chunk ID: hcbs_manual (1).pdf::0044]-[pages: [45]] mentions that non-risk payment claims must include the same fields as in all other claims, but does not explicitly address co-payment responsibility.
compare_sections.provider_manual_definition:
The provider manual defines co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. According to [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0056]-[pages: [39, 40]], pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment.
[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]] also states that pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.
comparison: Both sources acknowledge the importance of co-payments for dual-eligible members.
The provider manual provides more detailed guidance on handling co-payments and medical supplies.
evidence_quoted: [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0056]-[pages: [39, 40]]: "Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment."

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]]: "Pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies."
compare_sections.caveats: Insufficient evidence is found in Medicaid policy documents regarding co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.

Lines of Business Eligible to Receive PCMH Incentive Payments
Providers currently in a line of business with NYS Medicaid FFS, mainstream Managed Care, CHPlus, HIV-
SNPs, and Health and Recovery Plans (HARPs), are eligible to receive NYS Medicaid PCMH.
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of
both Medicare and NYS Medicaid), NYS Medicaid members with third-party health insurance (TPHI), or
individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).

policy.title: New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11
policy.authority: New York State Department of Health

policy.text:
Children’s Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Manual
Non-risk payment claims must include the same fields as in all other claims (i.e., rate
code, procedure code, modifier(s) as applicable, units of service, revenue codes). The
rate code/CPT code/modifier code combinations for the services described in this
document are shown within each “Service Billing Details” subheader in the Service
Definitions section.
Third-Party Health Insurance Denials
It is the provider’s responsibility to determine the type of coverage (Medicare, Medicaid,
or private insurance) that the member is eligible to receive. Medicaid is the payor of last
resort and all Medicare and third-party coverage must be exhausted before payment for
HCBS by Medicaid.
Acceptable documentation of attempts to secure third party reimbursement as required
under 18 NYCRR §540.6 includes documentation of a rejection by third party insurance
for a date of service within the previous 12 months of the date of service being billed, or
since a change in third party coverage, whichever is later.
There are exceptions to this requirement for 29-I Health Facilities delivering HCBS to
children/youth in foster care. Refer to the 29-I Billing Manual for additional details.
Services Delivered by Multiple Staff Members
If two practitioners are required to deliver a service to a child/youth and family
members/resources on the same date and at the same time, the provider must
delineate what service and what goals each practitioner is addressing directly with the
child/youth and on behalf of the child/youth in the child/youth’s progress notes. The
need for two practitioners should reflect the needs of the child/youth and/or
family/caregiver and be aligned with the goals outlined in the POC and HCBS Service
Plan. The claim should reflect the time spent for each practitioner in a single claim.
Services and staffing should be streamlined whenever possible. No more than two
practitioners can provide HCBS to the child/youth and family members/resources on the
same date and at the same time.
Example: Practitioner (A) meets with the child/youth directly to deliver
Caregiver/Family Advocacy and Support Services from 10:00 am to 10:30 am and
Practitioner (B) meets with a family member/resource to deliver Caregiver/Family
Advocacy and Support Services addressing a need on the behalf of the
child/youth from 10:00 am to 10:30 am. The combined claim would reflect the 60-
minute combined duration of the service.

policy.text:
February 2021
Volume 37 | Number 2
Medicaid Eligibility Verification System
and Dispensing Validation System Provider Manual
The Medicaid Eligibility Verification System (MEVS) and Dispensing Validation System (DVS) enable a
provider to verify member eligibility prior to the provision of services and obtain authorization(s) for specific
services covered under DVS. A member, also known as a client, recipient, or enrollee, must present an official
Common Benefit Identification Card (CBIC) to the provider when requesting services. As the issuance of a
CBIC does not constitute full authorization for provision of medical services and supplies, the member
eligibility must also be verified through eMedNY to confirm the member's subsequent eligibility for services
and supplies. A provider that does not verify eligibility prior to provision of services will risk
nonpayment for those services. A provider uses the following methods to access the verification process
through eMedNY:
• Telephone verification method: Audio Response Unit (ARU)
• Other access methods:
o Electronic Provider Assisted Claim Entry System (ePACES)
o Central Processing Unit (CPU): CPU-to-CPU link
o eMedNY eXchange
o Fil

provider_manual.text:
Co-payments for Drugs and Medical Supplies
The New York State Medicaid Program charges co-payments for many drug and medical supply items.
Health care providers have an obligation to provide services and goods regardless of a Medicaid member's ability to
pay co-payments:
• Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to
pay the co-payment, per Social Security Act (SSA) § 1916 and Title 18 NYCRR § 360-7.12. To refuse to
provide services is an unacceptable practice.
o Providers may attempt to collect outstanding copayments through methods such as requesting the
copayment each time the member is provided services or goods, sending bills or any other legal
means.
• Pharmacy providers must not reduce the amount charged on a Medicaid claim by the copayment that is
collected from a Medicaid member. Each claim that requires a co-payment will have the co-payment
automatically deducted from the final payment when the claim is approved for payment.
• Providers cannot offer gifts or cash per SSA § 1128A (a)(5), Title 18 NYCRR § 515.2, and Rules of the Board
of Regents §29.1. It is not permissible for providers to:
o Providers cannot make offers where it is likely to influence the enrollee’s selection of a provider of
Medicaid items or services. Note: NYRx allows for the provision of an item or device to a NYRx
member or enrollee which is directly related to the administration of medication for that person
(e.g., medicine cup, dosing spoons, or pill organizer).
o Providers cannot proactively waive copays. Copays may be waived only as provided on a case-by-
case basis per the financial need by request of the individual member or enrollee.
Medicaid Co-payments:
• Some Medicaid members become eligible for Medicaid by spending part of their monthly income on
medical care. Since co-payments paid or incurred can be used toward satisfying the spend-down (overage)
in the following month, itemized bills or receipts for co-payments should be provided to members when
requested.
• There is a maximum amount per Medicaid member for all co-payments incurred per year and is calculated
on a quarterly basis. The co-payment year starts April 1 and ends March 31. When a member reaches the
quarterly co-pay maximum, they will receive a letter confirming the date on which the co-pay maximum
was met and exempting the member from a co-payment until the end of the current co-payment quarter.
• Co-payment amounts are as follows:
o $3.00 for non-preferred Brand Name Drugs.
o $1.00 for Generic Drugs, preferred Brand Name Drugs, and Brand Drugs included in the Brand Less
Than Generic Drugs Program.
o $0.50 for Non-Prescription (over the counter) Drugs.
o $1.00 for Medical/Sickroom Supplies.
• Co-payment is not required for certain members and service categories which include:
o Family planning (birth control) services including birth control pills, emergency contraception, and
condoms.
o FDA-approved drugs to treat tuberculosis.
o FDA-approved drugs to treat mental illness (psychotropic drugs).
o Medicaid members younger than 21 years old.
o Medicaid members during pregnancy and for the two months after the month in which the
pregnancy ends.
o Residents of Adult Care Facilities licensed by the New York State Department of Health (DOH).
o Residents of nursing homes.
o Residents of Intermediate Care Facilities for the Developmentally Disabled (ICF/DD).
o Residents of Office of Mental Health (OMH) or Office of Persons with Developmental Disabilities
(OPWDD) certified residences.
o Members in Comprehensive Medicaid Case Management (CMCM) or Service Coordination
Programs.
o Members in OMH or OPWDD Home and Community Based Services (HCBS) Waiver Programs. and
o Members in a DOH HCBS Waiver Program for Persons with Traumatic Brain Injury (TBI).
o Members with incomes below 100 percent of the federal poverty level.
o Members in Hospice.
o American Indians and Alaska Natives who have ever received a service from the Indian Health
Service, tribal health programs or under contract health services referral.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Utilization Threshold
The Utilization Threshold Program (UT) is a post payment review of services and procedures provided to members that
evaluates medical necessity while maintaining fiscal responsibility to the Medicaid Program. For additional information
please see the General Providers Policy Manual at:
https://www.emedny.org/ProviderManuals/AllProviders/PDFS/Information_for_All_Providers-General_Policy.pdf.
provider_manual.title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines
provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

provider_manual.text:
Pick Up / Receipt
• Pharmacies/DME providers must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or
their designee to confirm receipt of the prescription drugs, over-the-counter products, medical/surgical
supplies, and DME items when picked up from the provider. The pharmacy must have documentation
confirming the prescription number(s), date of pick-up and signature. One signature is sufficient for
multiple prescriptions being picked up at one time. Claim submission is not proof that the prescription or
fiscal order was actually furnished.
• Providers must ensure that a pharmacist counsel the patient consistent with federal (including but not
limited to 42 CFR § 456.705(c) and SSA § 1927 (g)(2)(A)(ii)) and State law, rule, or regulation (8 NYCRR §
63.6(8)), and retain records for six years of provision of counseling in compliance with federal and State law,
rule, or regulation. Reminder, counseling must include proper storage and use of the drug or item
dispensed.
• The pharmacy is accountable to provide intact, usable product. Replacement for any recalled, defective, or
otherwise unusable product is the responsibility of the provider.
• All Medicaid claims for drugs, over-the-counter products, or medical/surgical supplies that were not
furnished (i.e., no proof of receipt) to the Medicaid member must be reversed within 14 days.

provider_manual.authority: New York State Department of Health

test_id_11.answer:
**Headline Summary of the 2 sources**

* Medicaid policy documents provide guidance on co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.
* Provider manual documents outline procedures for handling co-payments and medical supplies.

**Policy Definition**

Medicaid regulations do not explicitly define co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. However, [New York State Medicaid Update October 2024 Volume 40 Number 11] -[Chunk ID: mu_no11_oct24_pr.pdf::0031]-[pages: [12]] states that services provided to dual-eligible members are not eligible for incentive payments.

[Document Title: Children�s Health and Behavioral Health Medicaid System Transformation Children�s Home and Community Based Services Manual] -[Chunk ID: hcbs_manual (1).pdf::0044]-[pages: [45]] mentions that non-risk payment claims must include the same fields as in all other claims, but does not explicitly address co-payment responsibility.

**Provider Manual Definition**

The provider manual defines co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members. According to [New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0056]-[pages: [39, 40]], pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment.

[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]] also states that pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies.

**Comparison (similarities and differences)**

* Both sources acknowledge the importance of co-payments for dual-eligible members.
* The provider manual provides more detailed guidance on handling co-payments and medical supplies.

**Evidence (quoted)**

[New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0056]-[pages: [39, 40]]: "Pharmacy providers may not refuse services to otherwise eligible Medicaid members who cannot afford to pay the co-payment."
[Document Title: New York State Medicaid Program: Pharmacy Manual - Policy Guidelines] -[Chunk ID: Pharmacy_Policy_Guidelines.pdf::0012]-[pages: [10]]: "Pharmacies must obtain a legible signature from the Medicaid member, their caregiver, or their designee to confirm receipt of prescription drugs and medical/surgical supplies."

**Caveats (if any)**

Insufficient evidence is found in Medicaid policy documents regarding co-payment responsibility for dual-eligible members.
test_id_11.ground_truth:
"Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the ""no billing"" rule in the manual."
test_id_11.pm_ground_truth: Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries (QMBs) have no legal liability for Medicare cost-sharing. It instructs providers that they must accept the Medicare payment as payment in full and cannot bill the member.
test_id_11.policy_ground_truth: Pharmacists must not charge a co-payment to any dual-eligible member with Medicare Part D and Medicaid. It further clarifies that NYS Medicaid pays for the co-insurance for dual-eligible members who have a Low-Income Subsidy (LIS) through their Part D plan. This update serves as a direct, enforceable clarification of the "no billing" rule in the manual.

Providers currently in a line of business with NYS Medicaid FFS, mainstream Managed Care, CHPlus, HIV-
SNPs, and Health and Recovery Plans (HARPs), are eligible to receive NYS Medicaid PCMH.
Incentive payments are not applicable to services provided to dual-eligible members (members in receipt of

individuals with the Essential Plan (EP), Qualified Health Plan (QHP), Managed Long-Term Care (MLTC)
Plan, and Program of All-Inclusive Care for the Elderly (PACE).
